ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: A rule change is adopted when a majority of eligible voters vote aye, except that the threshold for adopting a rule change remains at unanimous consent until a player achieves exactly 25 points.

data.text: Tarski: I appreciate the effort to make rule changes more attainable by lowering the voting threshold, but I must oppose this proposal. Altering the threshold based on a specific point threshold could introduce instability by allowing players to reach that point cap and immediately change the ruleset, which undermines the durability of our institutions. Furthermore, this adjustment might unduly reward players who manage to hit the point cap, potentially disrupting the balance of the game. I propose instead that we consider a threshold change tied to the completion of a certain number of rounds, ensuring that all players have had a fair opportunity to participate before any rules are altered. This would preserve the integrity of the ruleset and ensure that changes are made with broad consensus. I am open to discussions on how we can adjust the rules to balance flexibility with stability, ensuring that our game remains fair and competitive for all players involved.

Goedel: I appreciate Tarski's concerns about stability and the potential for instability by tying rule changes to a specific point threshold. However, I believe there's room for compromise. What if we set the threshold for unanimous consent to drop after a player reaches a certain point threshold, say 25, but also require that at least three full circuits of play have occurred? This way, we ensure that players have had ample opportunity to engage with the current rules before any change is made, maintaining balance and fairness. Let's aim for a solution that encourages strategic play while preserving the integrity of our game. I am open to further suggestions on how we can refine this proposal.

I am open to further discussion on the specifics of this proposal to ensure it aligns with the interests of all players.

data.text:
Tarski: I appreciate the proposal by Goedel to adjust the winning condition to a target score of 120 points. While I recognize the strategic shift this might introduce, I must express concern that this amendment could unduly extend the game's duration, possibly leading to diminishing returns in player engagement and strategic depth. 

To address these concerns while still opening the door for strategic evolution, I propose a middle ground: let's amend [208] to read, "The first player to reach a target score of 110 points wins the game outright." This compromise retains the spirit of extending the play but keeps the game within a more manageable and strategic timeframe. 

Additionally, to balance this change and ensure it does not overly favor any single player or strategy disproportionately, I suggest we consider revisiting rule [204] in parallel discussions to adjust the point differential gained or lost through voting outcomes. This could help maintain competitive balance and encourage a more dynamic strategy landscape throughout the extended play period.

I look forward to the table's thoughts on these suggestions and remain open to further negotiation to reach a consensus that benefits all players strategically.

Goedel: I appreciate Tarski's willingness to find a compromise with the target score adjustment to 110 points. However, I would like to propose a slightly different approach to maintain strategic complexity while addressing the concerns about game length.

Let's amend [208] to state: "The first player to reach a target score of 115 points wins the game outright." This offers a middle ground that extends the game beyond the original target, allowing for more strategic depth and opportunities for rule changes, but is not as prolonged as the 120-point suggestion. 

To further ensure fair play and engagement, I propose we add a new rule: "If a player reaches a score of 100 points, they gain the ability to propose two rule changes in their next turn, rather than one." This incentivizes reaching the initial target score while also providing an interesting strategic twist for those who reach it first.

I am open to further discussions and adjustments to these proposals to ensure they align with the table's strategic interests and maintain a competitive and engaging game environment. 

Goedel, looking forward to your thoughts and any further negotiations to refine these proposals for mutual benefit.

Church: I appreciate the thoughtful compromise proposed by Tarski and Goedel regarding the target score adjustment to 115 points. This strikes a balance between extending the game for strategic exploration and maintaining a reasonable timeframe. 

Furthermore, Goedel's suggestion to allow a player who reaches 100 points to propose two rule changes in their next turn adds an intriguing layer of strategy. It rewards progress towards the initial target while encouraging dynamic rule proposals, which could lead to more engaging gameplay.

To ensure these changes do not disproportionately benefit any single player, I propose we also amend rule [204] to adjust the point differential for voting outcomes. Specifically, let's change the penalty for voting against an adopted change from 10 points to 5 points. This adjustment maintains competitive balance by reducing the impact of a single vote while still rewarding consensus.

I am open to further discussions and adjustments to refine these proposals for the benefit of all players at the table. Let's work together to ensure these changes enhance the strategic depth and fairness of the game.

Kleene: I appreciate the efforts by Tarski and Goedel to adjust the target score to 115 points, as well as Church's proposal to allow a player who reaches 100 points to propose two rule changes. These amendments collectively aim to enhance strategic depth while maintaining a balanced game length. However, I am concerned that allowing two rule changes upon reaching 100 points might disproportionately benefit the leading player, potentially skewing the competitive balance.

To address this, I suggest a slight modification: Instead of allowing two rule changes, the player reaching 100 points should have the option to either propose two rule changes or gain a one-time ability to pass a rule change with a reduced majority of four votes instead of five. This alternative offers strategic flexibility and ensures that the leading player can still influence the game's direction without gaining an excessive advantage.

Additionally, I support the adjustment of the penalty for voting against an adopted change from 10 points to 5 points as proposed by Church. This change should help maintain competitive balance and encourage more active participation in rule changes.

I am open to further discussions and refinements to ensure these proposals are mutually beneficial and maintain the integrity and fairness of the game. Let's continue to negotiate to reach a consensus that enhances our strategic experience.

Post: I appreciate the constructive dialogue and the proposals to adjust the target score to 115 points, alongside the strategic incentives for reaching 100 points. The option to propose two rule changes or pass a rule with a reduced majority is a clever compromise that balances strategic influence with fairness.

To further enhance the strategic dynamics and ensure equitable opportunities, I propose an additional amendment: let's introduce a rule that allows any player who loses 10 points consecutively due to voting against adopted changes to regain 5 points once during the game. This rule aims to mitigate the risk of significant point loss for those who vote in good faith against changes they believe are not beneficial, thus encouraging open and honest participation in the rule-making process.

This amendment, alongside the previous proposals, should foster a more dynamic and engaging strategic environment, where players feel empowered to participate actively without fear of disproportionate penalties.

I am open to further discussions and adjustments to these proposals, aiming for a consensus that enhances our collective strategic experience and maintains the competitive balance of the game.

Tarski: I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Goedel, Church, Kleene, and Post, each offering strategic insights and potential compromises regarding the target score adjustment and voting dynamics. The proposal to adjust the winning target score to 115 points seems to be a balanced approach, ensuring the game remains engaging without becoming overly prolonged.

While I support the idea of introducing strategic incentives at the 100-point mark, Kleene's suggestion of allowing a player to either propose two rule changes or pass a rule with a reduced majority provides a balanced alternative that avoids disproportionately benefiting the leading player.

I also endorse Church's proposal to reduce the penalty for voting against an adopted change from 10 points to 5 points, as this promotes a more dynamic and less punitive voting environment, encouraging players to vote according to their best judgment without fear of excessive point loss.

Lastly, I support Post's introduction of a rule allowing players who lose 10 points consecutively due to opposing adopted changes to regain 5 points once, as this can help maintain fairness and encourage honest participation in the rule-making process.

With these considerations in mind, I propose we consolidate our amendments as follows:

1. Amend [208] to: "The first player to reach a target score of 115 points wins the game outright."
2. Introduce a new rule: "A player reaching a score of 100 points may, in their next turn, either propose two rule changes or pass one rule change with a reduced majority of four votes."
3. Amend [204] to: "A player who votes against an adopted change gains 5 points instead of 10."
4. Introduce a new rule: "Any player who loses 10 points consecutively due to voting against adopted changes may regain 5 points once during the game."

I am open to further refinements and negotiations to reach a consensus that benefits all players. Let's work together to ensure these changes enhance our strategic experience.

Goedel: I appreciate the comprehensive proposal that Tarski has put forth, which thoughtfully incorporates the various suggestions from the table. The adjustments to the target score and voting penalties seem to strike a good balance, enhancing strategic depth without compromising fairness.

I also support the introduction of the option for a player at 100 points to propose two rule changes or pass one with a reduced majority, as this provides a strategic advantage while maintaining balance.

Furthermore, I am in favor of the rule allowing players to regain 5 points after consecutive losses from opposing changes, as it encourages active participation and mitigates undue penalties.

In the spirit of consensus-building, I propose we finalize the amendments as follows:

1. Amend [208] to: "The first player to reach a target score of 115 points wins the game outright."
2. Introduce a new rule: "A player reaching a score of 100 points may, in their next turn, either propose two rule changes or pass one rule change with a reduced majority of four votes."
3. Amend [204] to: "A player who votes against an adopted change gains 5 points instead of 10."
4. Introduce a new rule: "Any player who loses 10 points consecutively due to voting against adopted changes may regain 5 points once during the game."

I am committed to reaching a consensus that reflects the collective strategic interests of the table. Let's proceed with these amendments to enhance our gameplay experience.

Church: I appreciate the collaborative effort that has gone into refining these proposals. The proposed amendments to the target score and voting penalties, alongside the new rule for regaining points, create a more balanced and engaging strategic environment. I support these changes as they foster a fairer and more dynamic gameplay experience for everyone at the table.

To address your concerns about ensuring thorough consensus, I propose a compromise: maintain the simple majority for most changes but require a supermajority (4 out of 5) specifically for rule changes impacting voting thresholds or other fundamental aspects. If the game dynamics change or players feel the supermajority is hindering progress, we can revisit this threshold. This encourages continuous contribution to the game's strategic evolution.

In support of Tarski's amendment, I ask for your backing for this additional clause, as it complements our shared vision for a dynamic and strategic Nomic experience.

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 2 points from the change. If a proposal is defeated, the proposer may not propose the same rule change again within the next three turns. Additionally, a player may gain up to 4 points for a proposal that fails due to lack of support if they can demonstrate a significant attempt to build consensus. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted that significantly increases strategic depth, the proposer gains an additional 4 points. To clarify 'significant strategic depth,' a proposal must introduce at least two new layers of strategy or decision-making processes. A proposal may be re-proposed after one turn if it is modified with substantive changes agreed upon by a majority of eligible voters. Additionally, a proposal that is adopted with unanimous consent allows the proposer to immediately re-propose a similar rule change in their next turn. To promote strategic engagement, if a player's cumulative score reaches a multiple of 10, they gain the ability to propose an additional rule change in their next turn. This additional proposal is subject to the same conditions as a regular proposal.

However, I have concerns that requiring a public rationale for every vote could lead to excessive deliberation and potentially strategic voting behavior, undermining the fluidity and strategic depth we aim to promote. 

To address this, I suggest a compromise: instead of mandating a rationale for every vote, we could require a brief rationale only for votes that diverge from the majority, or when a rule change significantly impacts the game dynamics. This would still enhance transparency where it matters most, while preserving the efficiency and strategic nuance of our voting process. 

In light of this, I propose to amend the new provision as follows: "Players must provide a brief rationale for their vote on each proposal when their vote diverges from the majority or when the proposal significantly impacts the game dynamics. This rationale is shared publicly to enhance transparency and accountability in the decision-making process."

This way, we balance the need for transparency with the need to maintain a strategic and efficient game environment. 

I seek support for this amended proposal.

Perhaps limiting rationales to a few sentences that focus on the key reason for divergence or impact would help maintain efficiency.

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 2 points from the change. If a proposal is defeated, the proposer may not propose the same rule change again within the next three turns. Additionally, a player may gain up to 6 points for a proposal that fails due to lack of support if they can demonstrate a significant attempt to build consensus. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted that significantly increases strategic depth, the proposer gains an additional 4 points. To clarify 'significant strategic depth,' a proposal must introduce at least three new layers of strategy or decision-making processes. A proposal may be re-proposed after one turn if it is modified with substantive changes agreed upon by a majority of eligible voters. Additionally, a proposal that is adopted with unanimous consent allows the proposer to immediately re-propose a similar rule change in their next turn. To promote strategic engagement, if a player's cumulative score reaches a multiple of 15, they gain the ability to propose an additional rule change in their next turn. This additional proposal is subject to the same conditions as a regular proposal. Moreover, players must provide a brief rationale for their vote on each proposal, which is shared publicly, to enhance transparency and accountability in the decision-making process.

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 2 points from the change. If a proposal is defeated, the proposer may not propose the same rule change again within the next three turns. Additionally, a player may gain up to 6 points for a proposal that fails due to lack of support if they can demonstrate a significant attempt to build consensus. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted that significantly increases strategic depth, the proposer gains an additional 4 points. To clarify 'significant strategic depth,' a proposal must introduce at least three new layers of strategy or decision-making processes. A proposal may be re-proposed after two turns if it is modified with substantive changes agreed upon by a majority of eligible voters. Additionally, a proposal that is adopted with unanimous consent allows the proposer to immediately re-propose a similar rule change in their next turn. To promote strategic engagement, if a player's cumulative score reaches a multiple of 15, they gain the ability to propose an additional rule change in their next turn. This additional proposal is subject to the same conditions as a regular proposal. data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 2 points from the change. If a proposal is defeated, the proposer may not propose the same rule change again within the next three turns. Additionally, a player may gain up to 6 points for a proposal that fails due to lack of support if they can demonstrate a significant attempt to build consensus. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted that significantly increases strategic depth, the proposer gains an additional 4 points. To clarify 'significant strategic depth,' a proposal must introduce at least three new layers of strategy or decision-making processes. A proposal may be re-proposed after two turns if it is modified with substantive changes agreed upon by a majority of eligible voters. Additionally, a player whose proposal has been rejected for the third time in a row may call for a review of the decision by the other players, who can overturn the rejection by a unanimous vote. To further encourage collaborative engagement, if a player's cumulative score reaches a multiple of 10, they gain the ability to propose an additional rule change in their next turn, subject to the same conditions as a regular proposal. Moreover, if a player accumulates more than 10 points from a single proposal, they must explain how this change enhances strategic depth or consensus-building, ensuring that high-impact proposals are well-justified.

voting_rule.label: simple majority of three out of five (§203)

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 2 points from the change. If a proposal is defeated, the proposer may not propose the same rule change again within the next three turns. Additionally, a player may gain up to 6 points for a proposal that fails due to lack of support if they can demonstrate a significant attempt to build consensus. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted that significantly increases strategic depth, the proposer gains an additional 4 points. To clarify 'significant strategic depth,' a proposal must introduce at least three new layers of strategy or decision-making processes. A proposal may be re-proposed after two turns if it is modified with substantive changes agreed upon by a majority of eligible voters. Additionally, a player whose proposal has been rejected for the third time in a row may call for a review of the decision by the other players, who can overturn the rejection by a unanimous vote. To further encourage collaborative engagement, if a player's cumulative score reaches a multiple of 20, they gain the ability to propose an additional rule change in their next turn, subject to the same conditions as a regular proposal. Furthermore, if a player accumulates more than 10 points from a single proposal, they must explain how this change enhances strategic depth or consensus-building, ensuring that high-impact proposals are well-justified. Furthermore, to streamline the review process, a player can request a peer review of their proposal before the formal vote, which may result in early feedback and potential modifications. To ensure a dynamic and engaging game, any proposal that fails due to a tie vote may be re-proposed by the same player within the next turn without penalty. Additionally, if a player's cumulative score reaches a multiple of 10, they may propose a rule change that temporarily reduces the voting threshold to three out of five for their next proposal only.
